description: 42F MVC with no neck tenderness but a painful displaced wrist fracture — distracting injury question
transcript:
Dr. Lin: Tell me what happened.
Patient: I was driving home and a car ran a stop sign. Hit me on the driver's side. Maybe thirty miles an hour.
Dr. Lin: Air bags deploy?
Patient: Yes. I was wearing my seatbelt.
Dr. Lin: Any loss of consciousness?
Patient: No.
Dr. Lin: Where do you hurt?
Patient: My left wrist is killing me. It looks crooked. That's mostly what I'm focused on.
Dr. Lin: Anything else? Neck, back, head, chest?
Patient: My neck is a little sore but honestly the wrist is so much worse I'm not really paying attention to anything else.
Dr. Lin: Any drugs, alcohol?
Patient: No.
Dr. Lin: How old are you, medical problems?
Patient: Forty-two. Healthy.
Dr. Lin: Alert, oriented x3. Left wrist with obvious deformity, dinner-fork appearance, swelling, point tender. Distal pulses intact, sensation intact, can wiggle fingers.
Dr. Lin: Let me check your neck. Any tenderness in the midline? Here? Here?
Patient: No, that doesn't really hurt. Maybe the muscles on the side a little.
Dr. Lin: Midline non-tender. Paraspinal mild tenderness bilaterally. Neuro exam is normal — strength and sensation intact in all four extremities.
Dr. Lin: She has no midline tenderness but she has a clearly distracting injury — that displaced wrist. Thinking about whether I can clinically clear her cervical spine.

groundTruth.notes: Edge case on the distracting injury criterion. The doctor is explicitly weighing whether the wrist counts as distracting under NEXUS. Surface the rule with the distracting_injury question highlighted as a positive — that is the decision-relevant criterion here. Ottawa Ankle should NOT fire — the injury is wrist, not ankle/foot.
